articleTitle: Primary reverse total shoulder arthroplasty in patients aged ≤65 years: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
takeaways: This study analyzed reverse total shoulder arthroplasty (rTSA) outcomes in patients 65 years old and younger, representing a shift from the traditional use of this surgery only in elderly patients with low activity demands

The research examined contemporary evidence on complication rates and device longevity for younger rTSA patients, addressing previous concerns about using this surgical approach in more active individuals

The systematic review and meta-analysis methodology combined multiple studies to evaluate primary rTSA effectiveness specifically in the under-65 age group, providing stronger evidence than individual case studies
This represents an emerging trend where rTSA is being considered for younger adults despite historical reservations about durability and complications in higher-demand patients
